content.text: An RNN processes a sequence one token at a time — step 2 cannot start until step 1 finishes, because step 2 needs the hidden state produced by step 1. A transformer processes all positions
simultaneously in a single matrix multiply

. This single difference is what makes transformers trainable at scale: GPUs are designed to execute thousands of operations in parallel, and the transformer's attention mechanism maps directly onto batched matrix multiplication.

attrs.alt: RNN sequential processing vs transformer parallel attention, with complexity comparison table including state space models

content.text: An RNN cell computes a new hidden state from the current input and the previous hidden state:

attrs.latex: h_t = f(W_h h_{t-1} + W_x x_t + b)

content.text: creates a hard sequential chain. To compute
, and so on. No matter how many GPUs you have, you cannot parallelise this chain. Training on a sequence of length T requires T sequential steps.
Backpropagation through time (BPTT) faces the same problem in reverse: gradients flow from
sequentially. For long sequences this not only makes training slow but also leads to vanishing or exploding gradients, because the same weight matrix
is multiplied T times during backprop.

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import time

d_model, seq_len = 512, 512
batch = 8

# RNN: sequential over time
rnn = nn.GRU(d_model, d_model, batch_first=True).cuda()
x   = torch.randn(batch, seq_len, d_model, device='cuda')

torch.cuda.synchronize()
t0 = time.time()
for _ in range(100):
    out, _ = rnn(x)
torch.cuda.synchronize()
print(f'RNN:         {(time.time()-t0)*10:.1f} ms/iter')

# Transformer: parallel over sequence
attn = nn.MultiheadAttention(d_model, num_heads=8, batch_first=True).cuda()
mask = torch.tril(torch.ones(seq_len, seq_len, device='cuda')).bool()

torch.cuda.synchronize()
t0 = time.time()
for _ in range(100):
    out, _ = attn(x, x, x, attn_mask=~mask)
torch.cuda.synchronize()
print(f'Transformer: {(time.time()-t0)*10:.1f} ms/iter')
# Transformer is typically 5-20x faster at seq_len=512 on a modern GPU
In the transformer, the query–key dot products for all pairs of positions can be computed in a single batched matrix multiplication:

There is no recurrence — position 100's output does not depend on position 99's output having been computed first. Every position's output depends only on the input embeddings (which are all available before any computation starts) and the learned weight matrices. This means all T positions can be processed in parallel depth O(1).

During training with teacher forcing, all T target token predictions are computed in a single forward pass. The causal mask simply zeroes out future positions in the attention matrix — it does not introduce sequential computation. The whole batch of T predictions and T loss values is computed together.

import torch

T, d_model, d_k = 6, 512, 64

# All Q, K, V computed from input X in parallel
X = torch.randn(T, d_model)   # all T tokens available at once
Wq = torch.randn(d_model, d_k)
Wk = torch.randn(d_model, d_k)
Wv = torch.randn(d_model, d_k)

Q = X @ Wq   # (T, d_k) — all T queries at once
K = X @ Wk   # (T, d_k) — all T keys at once
V = X @ Wv   # (T, d_k) — all T values at once

# All T×T scores in one matmul — no loop over positions
scores = Q @ K.T / d_k**0.5   # (T, T)

# Causal mask — computed once, not sequentially
causal_mask = torch.tril(torch.ones(T, T, dtype=torch.bool))
scores = scores.masked_fill(~causal_mask, float('-inf'))
attn = torch.softmax(scores, dim=-1)
out  = attn @ V   # (T, d_k) — all T outputs at once
print(out.shape)  # (6, 64) — all 6 positions computed together

Parallelism is a training advantage only. At inference time, transformers become sequential again — they must generate one token at a time, and each new token requires re-running attention over the entire prefix. The
mitigates this by storing keys and values from previous tokens so they don't need to be recomputed — but the cache grows linearly with context length, consuming memory.

RNNs have the opposite profile: at inference they are O(1) per step with a fixed-size hidden state regardless of how much context has been processed. This is why RNN-like architectures (Mamba, RWKV, GLA) are an active area of research — they combine the parallel training of linear recurrences with the constant-cost inference of fixed hidden states.
Parallelism at Scale: Data, Model, and Pipeline
The transformer's parallelism over sequence positions is one of three axes used to scale LLM training across thousands of GPUs:
: different GPUs process different batches simultaneously and average gradients. Scales with number of GPUs linearly.
: split the weight matrices of individual attention heads or FFN layers across GPUs. Each GPU holds a shard of every layer.
: different GPUs hold different layers. Microbatches flow through the pipeline, keeping all stages active.
: split the sequence dimension across GPUs. Different GPUs attend to different token ranges, communicating KV states via ring all-reduce. Enables training on contexts too long to fit on one GPU.
Why parallelism is the decisive advantage:

GPT-3's 175B parameters were trained on roughly 300 billion tokens in a matter of weeks using thousands of A100s. The same training run with an LSTM would have taken orders of magnitude longer — not because LSTMs are less capable per operation, but because they cannot use the GPU's parallel processing units. The transformer didn't just make attention better; it made the whole of deep learning's hardware advantage available to sequence modelling.
You have access to many GPUs and want to exploit parallel matrix operations during training
Your sequences are short-to-medium length (context fits in GPU memory without KV cache issues)
Training throughput is the primary bottleneck — transformers saturate GPU compute far better than RNNs
You need bidirectional context access (BERT-style encoder) — RNNs require two separate passes for bidirectionality
Choose RNN-like or state-space models (Mamba, RWKV) when:
Inference latency and memory are critical constraints — fixed-size hidden state means O(1) per step, not O(T)
Sequences are very long (millions of tokens) where transformer KV cache becomes prohibitive
You are deploying on edge hardware with limited memory bandwidth
Online streaming tasks require constant-time processing regardless of how much history has been seen
: All T positions are processed in a single batched matrix multiply — modern A100/H100 GPUs can execute this at near-peak FLOPS, whereas sequential RNN steps leave most compute units idle.
: Parallelism across the sequence dimension enables tensor-, pipeline-, and data-parallel training across thousands of GPUs — enabling the 175B-parameter GPT-3 to train in weeks rather than years.
: Transformer attention directly attends to any position; gradients flow directly from output to input position without being multiplied through a long chain of recurrent weight matrices.
: Adding a causal mask does not introduce sequential computation — the same batched kernel handles any sequence length up to the maximum context window.
memory, limiting context length without tricks like FlashAttention, sliding-window attention, or sparse patterns.
: At generation time, token T+1 requires all previous T tokens' KV states. Parallelism is a training property only — autoregressive decoding cannot be trivially parallelized.
floats to the KV cache (L layers, H heads,
head dimension). A 70B-parameter LLM at 128K context requires tens of GB just for the cache.
: Tensor and pipeline parallelism require inter-GPU all-reduce operations at every layer. Communication can become the bottleneck on slower interconnects (PCIe vs. NVLink vs. InfiniBand).
Assuming transformers are parallel at inference
: Parallelism applies to training with teacher forcing. Autoregressive generation is sequential — do not confuse training and inference complexity.
Using RNNs when transformers can fit in memory
: If the sequence fits in GPU VRAM with a KV cache, a transformer will train 5-20x faster. RNNs are only preferable when memory or latency constraints are binding.
Ignoring sequence parallelism for long contexts
: For very long sequences, naive tensor parallelism is insufficient. Ring attention / sequence parallelism splits the sequence across GPUs — omitting this limits trainable context length.
matrix in HBM, causing memory-bandwidth bottlenecks. FlashAttention computes attention in tiles using SRAM, giving 2-4x speedup with identical outputs.
Use FlashAttention-2 or FlashAttention-3 for all transformer attention — it is a drop-in replacement with significant speedups
to fuse kernels and reduce Python overhead in the training loop
For multi-GPU training, profile communication vs. compute ratio — tensor parallelism works best on high-bandwidth NVLink; switch to pipeline parallelism on slower inter-node links
Use bf16 or fp8 mixed precision — half-precision doubles GPU throughput and halves memory without significant accuracy loss
Pad sequences to powers of 2 — hardware matrix units (Tensor Cores) operate most efficiently on aligned dimensions
: IO-aware exact attention implementation. Used in GPT-4, Llama 3, Mistral, and nearly all modern LLMs. Reduces attention from O(T²) memory to O(T) memory.
: Tensor and pipeline parallelism framework for training large transformers. Powers many of NVIDIA's and Microsoft's LLMs.
: ZeRO optimizer shards optimizer states, gradients, and parameters across GPUs — enables 100B+ parameter models on commodity GPU clusters.
: State-space and linear-attention models that achieve parallel training via parallel prefix scan while keeping O(1) inference. An active research frontier.
: Inference engine for transformers using PagedAttention — manages KV cache as virtual memory pages to maximize GPU utilization during parallel decoding.
: The softmax in attention can overflow with large logits. Always divide by
(scaling factor) and use numerically stable softmax implementations that subtract the max before exponentiation.
: The KV cache and attention matrix can silently OOM. Use gradient checkpointing during training and monitor peak VRAM; set explicit context length limits in production.

: All-reduce operations in data-parallel training require all ranks to participate. If one rank fails or hangs, the entire job deadlocks. Use watchdog timeouts and fault-tolerant training checkpointing.
How Attention Parallelism Works: A Concrete Walkthrough
The self-attention mechanism is the engine of transformer parallelism. Understanding
which operations run in parallel — and which remain sequential — is essential before writing distributed training code.
The Three Matrices: Q, K, V

For a token sequence of length T with model dimension d, attention begins by projecting the input embedding matrix X ∈ ℝ^{T×d} into three separate spaces simultaneously. Because each projection is an independent matrix multiply, they can all run at the same time on a GPU:

attrs.latex: Q = X W_Q,\quad K = X W_K,\quad V = X W_V,\quad W_{*} \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d_k}

content.text: Then scaled dot-product attention scores for every token pair are computed at once — a T×T matrix multiply:
The key insight: the entire T×T score matrix and the final V-weighted sum are computed with two large matrix multiplications, which cuBLAS/cuDNN execute on thousands of CUDA cores simultaneously.
NumPy Implementation Showing the Parallel Structure

import numpy as np

def scaled_dot_product_attention(Q, K, V, mask=None):
    """
    Q, K, V: (T, d_k) — all tokens simultaneously
    Returns: (T, d_v) attended output
    """
    d_k = Q.shape[-1]

    # Step 1: compute all T*T scores in one matmul (fully parallel)
    scores = Q @ K.T / np.sqrt(d_k)   # (T, T)

    # Optional causal mask for autoregressive models
    if mask is not None:
        scores = scores + mask * -1e9  # -inf on future positions

    # Step 2: softmax over key dimension (row-wise, vectorised)
    scores = scores - scores.max(axis=-1, keepdims=True)  # numerical stability
    attn_weights = np.exp(scores) / np.exp(scores).sum(axis=-1, keepdims=True)  # (T, T)

    # Step 3: weighted sum of values (another matmul, still fully parallel)
    output = attn_weights @ V  # (T, d_v)
    return output, attn_weights

# ── Toy demo ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
T, d_model, d_k = 6, 16, 8

np.random.seed(0)
X = np.random.randn(T, d_model)       # 6 token embeddings
Wq = np.random.randn(d_model, d_k)
Wk = np.random.randn(d_model, d_k)
Wv = np.random.randn(d_model, d_k)

Q, K, V = X @ Wq, X @ Wk, X @ Wv    # all three projections happen independently
causal_mask = np.triu(np.ones((T, T)), k=1)  # upper triangle = future tokens

out, weights = scaled_dot_product_attention(Q, K, V, causal_mask)
print(f"Output shape: {out.shape}")         # (6, 8)
print(f"Attention weights (row 2): {weights[2].round(3)}")
# Row 2 can only attend to tokens 0, 1, 2 → future positions have ~0 weight
Compare this with an RNN step, which requires the hidden state
— a strict sequential dependency that prevents batching across time steps.
Implementing Data Parallelism with PyTorch DDP
Data parallelism (DP) replicates the full model on every GPU and splits each mini-batch across replicas. After the forward pass, gradients are averaged via an
collective before the optimizer step. PyTorch's
(DDP) does this automatically and overlaps gradient communication with the backward pass — a key performance optimisation.

# train_ddp.py  —  launch with:
#   torchrun --nproc_per_node=4 train_ddp.py
import os, torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.distributed as dist
from torch.nn.parallel import DistributedDataParallel as DDP
from torch.utils.data import DataLoader, TensorDataset
from torch.utils.data.distributed import DistributedSampler

# ── 1. Initialise the process group ──────────────────────────────────────────
dist.init_process_group(backend="nccl")           # "gloo" on CPU
rank       = dist.get_rank()                       # 0 … world_size-1
world_size = dist.get_world_size()
local_rank = int(os.environ["LOCAL_RANK"])
torch.cuda.set_device(local_rank)
device = torch.device("cuda", local_rank)

# ── 2. Minimal transformer-style FFN block ────────────────────────────────────
class FeedForward(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, d_model=256, d_ff=1024):
        super().__init__()
        self.net = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(d_model, d_ff),
            nn.GELU(),
            nn.Linear(d_ff, d_model),
        )
        self.norm = nn.LayerNorm(d_model)

    def forward(self, x):
        return self.norm(x + self.net(x))   # residual + norm

class TinyTransformerBlock(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, d_model=256, nhead=4, d_ff=1024):
        super().__init__()
        self.attn = nn.MultiheadAttention(d_model, nhead, batch_first=True)
        self.ff   = FeedForward(d_model, d_ff)
        self.norm = nn.LayerNorm(d_model)

    def forward(self, x):
        attn_out, _ = self.attn(x, x, x)
        x = self.norm(x + attn_out)
        return self.ff(x)

model = TinyTransformerBlock(d_model=256, nhead=4, d_ff=1024).to(device)

# ── 3. Wrap with DDP — gradient sync happens automatically ────────────────────
model = DDP(model, device_ids=[local_rank])

# ── 4. Synthetic dataset + DistributedSampler (no duplicate batches across GPUs)
N, T_seq, D = 2048, 16, 256
X = torch.randn(N, T_seq, D)
y = torch.randn(N, T_seq, D)
dataset = TensorDataset(X, y)
sampler = DistributedSampler(dataset, num_replicas=world_size, rank=rank, shuffle=True)
loader  = DataLoader(dataset, batch_size=32, sampler=sampler)

# ── 5. Training loop ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
optimiser = torch.optim.AdamW(model.parameters(), lr=1e-3)
loss_fn   = nn.MSELoss()

for epoch in range(3):
    sampler.set_epoch(epoch)          # ensures different shuffle each epoch
    for xb, yb in loader:
        xb, yb = xb.to(device), yb.to(device)
        pred = model(xb)
        loss = loss_fn(pred, yb)
        optimiser.zero_grad()
        loss.backward()               # DDP: gradients are AllReduced here
        optimiser.step()

    if rank == 0:
        print(f"Epoch {epoch+1}/3  loss={loss.item():.4f}")

dist.destroy_process_group()
Critical points in the code above:
ensures each GPU sees a different shard of data per epoch — without it, every GPU trains on identical batches, wasting compute.
and overlaps with gradient computation (pipelined communication).
guard prevents duplicate logging and checkpoint saves.
For model parallelism (model too large for one GPU), replace DDP with Tensor Parallel (Megatron-style) or Pipeline Parallel (DeepSpeed).
As context windows grow — GPT-4 at 128k tokens, Gemini at 1M tokens — the T×T attention matrix no longer fits in a single GPU's VRAM. Two newer techniques split the sequence dimension across GPUs:

Instead of replicating LayerNorm and Dropout across all tensor-parallel ranks, Megatron-LM partitions the sequence dimension for these non-attention operations. Each GPU holds a contiguous chunk of token representations. AllGather + ReduceScatter collectives stitch results together at layer boundaries.

For sequences too long to fit even a single attention head on one GPU, Ring Attention distributes the K and V matrices around a logical ring of GPUs while each GPU computes attention over its local Q block. Blocks are passed one hop at a time while the local computation runs, hiding most communication latency:

attrs.latex: \text{Each GPU } i: \quad \text{Attn}(Q_i, K_{\text{ring}}, V_{\text{ring}}) \text{ accumulated as KV rotates}

content.text: Real-World Example: FlashAttention and IO-Aware Parallelism
FlashAttention (Dao et al., 2022) demonstrated that raw FLOP count is not the only bottleneck. Standard attention performs O(T²) reads and writes between GPU HBM (slow) and SRAM (fast), making it

FlashAttention tiles the Q, K, V matrices into blocks that fit in SRAM, computes partial softmax statistics online without materialising the full T×T matrix, and accumulates the result. The algorithm is mathematically identical to standard attention but achieves:
2–4× wall-clock speedup on A100 GPUs for sequences ≥ 1k tokens
Linear VRAM scaling in sequence length (no T×T matrix stored)
Exact (not approximate) results — unlike sparse or linear attention variants

# PyTorch ≥ 2.0 ships Flash Attention natively via scaled_dot_product_attention
import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F

def flash_attention_demo(batch=2, heads=8, T=4096, d_k=64):
    device = "cuda" if torch.cuda.is_available() else "cpu"
    dtype  = torch.float16 if device == "cuda" else torch.float32

    Q = torch.randn(batch, heads, T, d_k, device=device, dtype=dtype)
    K = torch.randn(batch, heads, T, d_k, device=device, dtype=dtype)
    V = torch.randn(batch, heads, T, d_k, device=device, dtype=dtype)

    # PyTorch auto-selects FlashAttention kernel when available
    with torch.backends.cuda.sdp_kernel(
        enable_flash=True,
        enable_math=False,    # disable naive O(T^2) fallback
        enable_mem_efficient=False
    ):
        out = F.scaled_dot_product_attention(Q, K, V, is_causal=True)

    print(f"Output: {out.shape}, device: {out.device}, dtype: {out.dtype}")
    return out

# Run demo (executes on CUDA if available, CPU float32 otherwise)
flash_attention_demo()
flag constructs the causal mask inside the kernel, avoiding the overhead of allocating and applying a T×T boolean tensor in Python — another source of the speedup.

At 32k tokens, standard attention runs out of memory on a 80 GB GPU; FlashAttention completes in 29 ms with 128 MB. This is why virtually every long-context model — Llama 3, Mistral, Phi-3 — uses FlashAttention by default.
, creating a hard chain that no GPU parallelism can break.
Transformers are fully parallel during training
attention scores are computed in a single batched matrix multiply; no sequential dependency exists.
Parallelism is a training advantage only
: At inference time, transformers generate one token at a time — the KV cache mitigates recomputation but inference is still sequential.
Three parallelism axes for large-scale training
: Data parallelism (batches), tensor/model parallelism (weight matrices), and pipeline parallelism (layers) are combined to saturate thousands of GPUs.
State Space Models (Mamba) combine both strengths
: Parallel prefix-scan training AND O(1) inference — an active frontier that may challenge transformers for long-context tasks.
: Nearly all modern LLMs use FlashAttention to reduce the
memory cost and gain 2-4x speed improvements.
The transformer's parallelism is why modern LLMs exist
: GPT-3 was only trainable in weeks because transformers map onto GPU matrix units; the equivalent LSTM would have taken years.
